title: Round challah for Rosh Hashanah
subtitle: Sweet, raisined, coiled into a round for the new year
excerpt: A sweet enriched challah coiled into a round crown shape, studded with raisins to signal a sweet new year. The dough takes egg yolks, honey, and oil and bakes to a deep mahogany with a tight, pull-apart crumb.
sourceNotes: Round challah is the traditional form for Rosh Hashanah, distinct from the braided plaited loaf made for Shabbat. The coiled shape represents the cyclical nature of the year. Pre-1920 American Jewish community cookbooks document the round form and the inclusion of raisins as a marker of sweetness for the holiday. The egg-yolk-and-oil enrichment is characteristic of Ashkenazi challah tradition.

content.text: The round is the Rosh Hashanah loaf, replacing the familiar plaited braid. Coiled into a crown and sweet with honey and raisins, it carries a specific meaning: the year turning on itself, a complete circuit. The dough here is on the richer end of the challah spectrum, with a higher egg-yolk count than a Shabbat loaf and enough honey to bring a gentle sweetness to every bite.

attrs.prepNote: soaked in warm water for 15 minutes, drained
beaten with 1 tbsp water, for glazing

content.text: Combine the flour, yeast, and salt in a large bowl. Whisk the honey, oil, egg yolks, and whole egg into the warm water until combined. Pour the wet ingredients into the flour and mix to a dough. Turn out and knead for 12 minutes until smooth, satiny, and elastic. The dough will be slightly firm at first as it contains less water than a lean dough; it softens with work. Add the drained raisins in the final minute of kneading, folding them in evenly.

Place the dough in an oiled bowl, cover with a damp cloth, and leave until doubled in volume. Honey and oil in the dough slow the fermentation slightly; a warm kitchen (23 to 25°C) gives about 75 minutes. The dough is ready when it holds a finger poke without springing back immediately.

Line a large baking tray with baking paper. On an unfloured surface, roll the dough into a rope about 80 cm long; it should taper only slightly at the ends. Starting from one end, coil it tightly around itself in a spiral, tucking the outer end underneath the loaf. The
should produce a round about 22 cm across, sitting up on the tray rather than spreading flat. Slide onto the lined tray.

Cover loosely with oiled cling film and leave to prove for 45 minutes until noticeably puffed. Brush generously with the egg-yolk glaze, taking care to reach all the curved surfaces. Scatter sesame seeds across the top if using. Apply the glaze again after 5 minutes for a deep, even colour.

Preheat the oven to 190°C conventional. Bake for 32 to 36 minutes until the loaf is a deep mahogany brown; the base should sound hollow when tapped, and an internal probe should read 93 to 96°C. If the top colours before the centre is baked through, lay a sheet of foil loosely over it for the last 10 minutes. Cool on a wire rack for at least 30 minutes before slicing.
